1: "This is the word that came to Jeremiah from the LORD" — the standard reception formula introduces the potter's house sign-act, one of the most Christologically resonant narratives in Jeremiah. The prophetic word that comes to Jeremiah in this chapter eventually points toward the Word made flesh, who himself is both the Potter and the vessel made new.
2: "Get up and go down to the potter's house" — the divine directive sends Jeremiah to observe a craftsman at work. The potter's house as the site of prophetic revelation anticipates the NT theme of encountering the divine in ordinary human labor and material. The incarnation itself is God "going down" to the place of human craftsmanship to speak through the lowliest of materials — flesh and clay.
3: "He was there, working at the wheel (ʾōbānāyim)." The image of the potter at the wheel — present, attentive, hands on the clay — is a picture of engaged, personal sovereignty. Christologically, this is the posture of the incarnate Son: not distant oversight but hands-on, present involvement in human material. The Word becoming flesh (John 1:14) is the ultimate going-down to the potter's wheel.
4: "The clay vessel he was shaping was ruined in his hands; so he reworked it into another vessel, as seemed best to him." The key Christological content of the sign-act: the clay that does not cooperate is not discarded but remade. The sovereignty exercised here is not destructive but reformative. Christologically, this is the logic of the new covenant: Israel's failure under the Sinai covenant does not end YHWH's purpose but leads to the new covenant's reformation of the same material. 2 Corinthians 5:17 ("if anyone is in Christ, he is a new creation; the old has passed away, behold the new has come") is the NT statement of the potter's reforming work applied to individuals through union with Christ.
5: "Then the word of the LORD came to me" — the transition from observation to divine interpretation. The prophetic sign-act is incomplete without the divine word that interprets it; the sign is the vehicle, the word is the content. Christologically, Jesus is both sign and word simultaneously: he is the sign of God's reign and also the one who interprets that sign with absolute authority (John 7:16-17).
6: "Can I not deal with you as this potter has dealt with the clay, O house of Israel?" The rhetorical question assumes its own answer — YHWH's potter-sovereignty over Israel is absolute and non-negotiable. Romans 9:20-21 cites this exact imagery: "Will what is molded say to its molder, 'Why have you made me like this?' Has the potter no right over the clay?" Paul deploys the potter-clay argument in the context of Israel's rejection and the inclusion of Gentiles — the very context of Jeremianic prophecy. Christ himself is the supreme vessel formed by the Father, and through union with him, believers become what Paul calls "God's workmanship (poiēma), created in Christ Jesus for good works" (Ephesians 2:10) — the Greek poiēma (workmanship/poem/crafted thing) echoes the potter-craft imagery.
7: "There are times when I pronounce concerning a nation or kingdom that I will uproot it, tear it down, and destroy it." The conditional character of prophetic judgment — YHWH reserves the right to revoke announced judgment — is here stated as a universal principle governing all nations. Christologically, this conditionality reflects the divine character that reaches its fullest expression in the cross: God could have executed the irreversible judgment of Jeremiah 19's broken flask against all of humanity, but instead entered history in Christ to provide the path of repentance (v8).
8: "But if that nation turns from its evil, I will relent concerning the disaster I intended to bring on it." The repentance-and-relenting principle is the most explicit OT statement of divine responsiveness to human turning. 2 Peter 3:9 ("not wishing that any should perish, but that all should reach repentance") is the NT formulation of this same divine disposition. Christologically, the cross is God's definitive act of opening a way of repentance where the demands of justice seemed to close every door. The "relenting" of v8 is ultimately grounded in Christ's atoning work — the justice that must be met is met, enabling the mercy that was always the divine desire.
9: "And there are times when I declare concerning a nation or kingdom that I will build and plant it" — the positive side of the conditional: YHWH is not only the God who withholds judgment but the one who announces prosperity, and that announcement too is contingent on human response. Christologically, the unconditional new covenant (Jeremiah 31:31-34) supersedes this conditionality for the covenant community: in Christ, the building-and-planting is secured by the divine promise rather than contingent on human performance (Hebrews 6:13-20).
10: "But if that nation does evil in my sight and does not obey me, I will relent of the good I said I would do for it." The symmetry of vv7-10 — conditionality works in both directions — establishes the relational, rather than mechanical, character of divine governance. Christologically, the new covenant removes this reverse-conditionality for those in Christ: Romans 8:38-39 declares nothing can separate believers from God's love in Christ Jesus. The conditionality of vv7-10 belongs to the old covenant order; the new covenant's unconditional security is grounded in Christ's permanent standing before the Father.
11: "I am shaping disaster against you right now and devising a plan against you. Turn back, each of you from your evil way." YHWH as active, present Potter reshaping the clay of judgment — the present-tense urgency. The call to turn back is the OT form of the gospel's call to repentance. Christologically, Jesus opens his public ministry with the same urgent call: "The kingdom of God is at hand; repent and believe in the gospel" (Mark 1:15). The potter's "right now" is the same urgency Paul expresses: "Behold, now is the favorable time; behold, now is the day of salvation" (2 Corinthians 6:2).
12: "But they say: It is useless! We will follow our own plans and act according to the stubborn evil of our own hearts." The response of defiant self-determination — refusing the potter's reforming hand — is the posture that makes judgment irreversible. Christologically, this defiance is precisely what Jesus laments over Jerusalem (Matthew 23:37: "How often would I have gathered your children together... and you were not willing!"). The refusal of the reforming hand is the form of human sin that the cross addresses: Christ does not force the clay but enters into the consequence of clay that refused to be remade.
13: "Ask around among the nations — who has ever heard anything like this?" The unprecedented nature of Israel's apostasy — more shocking than any pagan behavior — is the inverse of Israel's unique covenant calling. Christologically, Jesus uses similar comparative logic: "It will be more tolerable on the day of judgment for the land of Sodom than for you" (Matthew 11:24). Those who have received more revelation bear greater responsibility — the unique horror of Israel's rejection of YHWH finds its climactic expression in the rejection of YHWH's own Son.
14: "Does the snow of Lebanon ever leave the mountain crags? Do the cold streams ever run dry?" The natural world's consistency serves as the contrasting measure for Israel's inconstancy. Snow remains on Lebanon; streams maintain their course; but Israel abandoned the living water (Jeremiah 2:13). Christologically, the natural constancy that Israel violated finds its answer in Christ, who is "the same yesterday and today and forever" (Hebrews 13:8). The reliable streams that Israel did not learn from are the OT image of Christ's own unchanging faithfulness.
15: "Yet my people have forgotten me; they burn offerings to worthless idols. They have stumbled off the ancient paths." The ancient paths (nĕtîḇôt ʿôlām) from which Israel has strayed are the covenant ways of YHWH. Christologically, Christ presents himself as the path from which no true disciple strays: "I am the way" (John 14:6). The stumbling off the ancient path in Jeremiah 18:15 anticipates the NT's invitation back to the eternal way through the one who is himself the way.
16: "They have made their land a desolation, a thing to be hissed at forever." The desolation-formula (šammâ) appears here as the self-caused consequence of following the wrong paths. Christologically, the hissing that passes by Jerusalem's desolation anticipates the mocking at the cross: those who passed by "wagged their heads" and mocked (Matthew 27:39-40; Psalms 22:7). The desolation of Jerusalem and the desolation of the crucified Christ are typologically linked — in both cases the mockery of passersby reveals the depth of the covenant people's shame.
17: "Like a scorching east wind I will scatter them before the enemy; I will show them my back, not my face, in the day of their calamity." The divine ʿōrep (back, neck) turned toward Israel — YHWH withdrawing his face — is the covenant curse of abandonment. Christologically, the cry of dereliction at the cross (Matthew 27:46: "My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?") is the ultimate instance of the divine face being hidden. Christ experiences the full covenant curse of the hidden face — which Israel's sin had earned — so that those who trust him are never forsaken (Hebrews 13:5: "I will never leave you nor forsake you").
18: "Come, let us make plans against Jeremiah." The conspiracy of the religious and intellectual establishment against the faithful prophet directly prefigures the conspiracy against Jesus. Jeremiah's opponents cite the institutional guarantors of truth: "instruction will still come from the priests, counsel from the wise, and a word from the prophets" — implying that Jeremiah is expendable because the institutions will continue without him. Jesus's enemies use the same institutional reasoning (John 11:48-50: "the Romans will come and take away both our place and our nation\... it is better that one man die for the people"). The prophetic victim of institutional conspiracy is the OT type; Christ is the fulfillment.
19: "Give attention to me, O LORD; listen to what my adversaries are saying." The Fifth Confession opens (vv19-23) with Jeremiah's appeal for divine attention to his suffering. The prophet who pleaded for the people now pleads for himself against those who repay his intercession with persecution. Christologically, the pattern of intercession repaid with conspiracy is the pattern of Christ's passion: he prayed for his people (John 17; Luke 23:34) and was killed for it. Psalms 35:12 ("They repay me evil for good") and Psalms 109:4 ("in return for my love they accuse me") are the same pattern that Jeremiah voices here.
20: "Should good be paid back with evil? Yet they have dug a pit for my life." The pit (šûḥâ) dug for Jeremiah echoes Psalms 7:15; Jeremiah 35:7; Jeremiah 57:6 — the conspiracy to trap and kill the innocent. "Remember how I stood before you and pleaded for them" — the intercessory faithfulness that is repaid with murderous conspiracy. Christologically, Jesus's greatest act of intercession (the cross) is the act by which his conspirators believed they were eliminating him. The pit dug for Jeremiah is the OT type of the tomb prepared for Christ — in both cases the pit becomes the place from which YHWH/God delivers (Psalms 16:10; Acts 2:31).
21: "Therefore hand their children over to famine... let their men be struck down by plague." The imprecatory prayer of vv21-23 is Jeremiah's most extreme call for judgment on his enemies. This is the most difficult text in the Confessions for Christian readers. Christologically, it is read as the voice of the old covenant justice-demand, which Christ absorbs and redirects at the cross: rather than imprecating judgment on others, Christ absorbs judgment for others (Luke 23:34: "Father, forgive them, for they do not know what they are doing"). The imprecation of Jeremiah 18:21 and the intercession of Luke 23:34 stand as the polar contrast between old-covenant prophetic response and Christ's new-covenant response to murderous conspiracy.
22: "Let a cry be heard from their houses when you bring marauders against them without warning; for they have dug a pit to trap me and laid snares for my feet." The pit and snare imagery recalls the psalmic tradition of the righteous sufferer (Ps 22; 35; 69). Christologically, the pit/snare complex surrounding the faithful prophet is a type of the suffering of the Righteous One that finds its fullest form in Christ's passion. Ps 69 — one of the most heavily cited psalms in the passion narratives — uses precisely this language (Psalms 69:2: "I sink in deep mire where there is no foothold").
23: "But you, O LORD, know all their schemes against my life. Do not forgive their guilt or erase their sin from your sight." The closing imprecation — a stark contrast to Jesus's "Father, forgive them" — marks the limit of the old covenant prophetic response. Paul addresses this limit directly in Romans 12:19-21 ("Beloved, never avenge yourselves, but leave it to the wrath of God") and Matthew 5:44 ("Love your enemies and pray for those who persecute you") is Jesus's own surpassing of the old covenant standard. Christ fulfills Jeremiah's prophetic office at this point not by repeating the imprecation but by transforming it into intercession — the new covenant's definitive answer to the problem of unjust suffering.
